rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical formal C-C cleavage of 1,3-diaryl-1,3-diketones with aryl alkenes and water to give 1,4-ketoalcohol benzoate esters via anodic oxidation
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation: deprotonated 1,3-diketone enolate is oxidized at the anode to an α-keto C-centered radical; a second anodic oxidation converts the alkene-adduct radical to a benzylic cation; cathode reduces protons/water to H2

key_intermediates.name: benzylic C-radical adduct (III) from radical addition to styrene
benzylic alcohol intermediate (V) after water trapping
dimer 7 from coupling of α-keto radicals (in absence of alkene)

electron_flow: Base/anode deprotonates and oxidizes the 1,3-diketone to an α-keto radical, which adds to the styrene to give a benzylic radical; a second anodic oxidation forms a benzylic cation that is trapped by water, and the resulting hemiketal rearranges with C-C cleavage to deliver the 1,4-ketoalcohol ester while the cathode evolves H2.

probe_reagents.name: TEMPO
probe_reagents.observation: Reaction strongly inhibited; TEMPO adduct 6 of the α-keto radical isolated in 44% yield, supporting an α-keto C-radical intermediate

probe_reagents.name: Omission of alkene (styrene 2a)
probe_reagents.observation: Dimer 7 of two α-keto radicals isolated in 23%, confirming generation of the α-keto radical

probe_reagents.name: H2 18-O labeled water
probe_reagents.observation: 18O is incorporated into the ester/alcohol of 3a (HRMS), showing water is the oxygen source

probe_reagents.name: Cyclic voltammetry of 1a vs 2a
probe_reagents.observation: 1a oxidizes at 2.03 V (vs SCE), lower than styrene 2a at 2.20 V, so the diketone is preferentially oxidized at the anode

notes: Authors note that concerted proton-coupled electron transfer (loss of H+ and e- simultaneously) from 1a cannot be fully excluded as an alternative to stepwise deprotonation/oxidation to form the α-keto radical (II). Substrate scope (failure of 3ae-3ah) suggests aryl substitution on both diketone and alkene is required, consistent with stabilization of radical/cation intermediates.
